result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting, because I think it's one of the most underexamined experiences in human life. Not the dramatic waiting — not the waiting for biopsy results or the waiting at the airport when someone you love is finally coming home — but the ordinary, ambient waiting that fills in the gaps between things. The waiting that doesn't announce itself. The waiting that you only notice, if you notice it at all, as a kind of low hum beneath everything else.

Consider the moment before a kettle boils. You've set the water on, and now you're standing in your kitchen, and you are technically free to do something else, go check your phone or sort through the mail or look out the window at whatever is happening in the street. But instead you stay. You stand in front of the kettle. You watch the small window on the side where, if the kettle has one, you can see the water beginning to tremble. Why do you stay? The kettle doesn't need you there. The water will boil regardless. And yet something keeps you in that spot, in that posture of attention, as though your witnessing the process is part of the process. As though the tea won't taste right if you weren't there for its beginning.

I think this reveals something true about how we actually experience time, which is nothing like how we describe it. We describe time as a river, as an arrow, as something that flows or passes or marches forward. We talk about it as though we are standing on a bank watching it go by, observers of an external phenomenon. But the waiting at the kettle doesn't feel like that. It feels like you are inside something that hasn't decided yet what it's going to become. The water is neither cold nor boiling. It is becoming. And you, standing there, are also somehow in a state of becoming, held in suspension between the moment you decided you wanted tea and the moment you'll actually have it. The waiting is not empty time. It's a different kind of full.

---

There's a word in Portuguese — *saudade* — that everyone reaches for when they want to demonstrate that some languages contain emotional experiences that others can't fully name. It means something like longing, but longing for something that may never have existed in quite the form you're longing for, or longing for something that is irrecoverable, a place or a feeling or a version of yourself. Everyone knows about *saudade*. It's become almost a cliché of the "untranslatable words" genre that floats around the internet.

But I want to talk about a different Portuguese word, less famous, that I find more interesting: *madrugada*. It refers to the hours between midnight and dawn — not night exactly, and not yet morning. Those hours have a quality to them that any single word in English fails to capture. We might say "the small hours" or "the dead of night" or "before dawn," but none of those quite get at what *madrugada* means, which is something closer to: the part of the night that belongs to no one, that exists outside the ordinary categories of sleeping and waking, that is its own world.

I've been awake in the *madrugada* more times than I can count. Most people have. There's a specific texture to being awake at 3 a.m. that is genuinely unlike being awake at any other hour. The quality of silence is different. Even in cities, even in places that claim to never sleep, 3 a.m. has a silence that is less like the absence of sound and more like sound's negative space — you become aware of the underlying quiet that noise is always covering up. The refrigerator hum. The distant particular sound of your own thoughts, which seem louder and stranger and more authoritative than they do in the daytime.

And there's a particular kind of thinking that only happens in the *madrugada*. Problems seem both more serious and more soluble. The catastrophic thought and the sudden clarity live right next to each other. I have made more genuine resolutions at 3 a.m. than at any other hour. I have also entertained more irrational fears. The *madrugada* doesn't discriminate between the profound and the distorted — it amplifies both equally, turning up the gain on whatever's running in the background of your mind.

What I find fascinating about this is that it's essentially a form of waiting — waiting for morning, even if you're not consciously aware of that's what you're doing. The *madrugada* has that same quality as the kettle: you're inside something unresolved. Dawn hasn't decided to come yet. The day hasn't chosen its shape. You exist in a kind of atmospheric parenthesis.

---

I want to pivot here, because I think there's something about waiting that connects to a question I find genuinely difficult, which is the question of how much of experience is structure versus content.

Let me try to say what I mean by that.

When people talk about having a meaningful life, or about happiness, they almost always talk in terms of content — the things that fill a life. The relationships, the work, the pleasures, the achievements, the memories. What did you do? Who did you love? What did you make or build or understand? These are content questions. And they're obviously important. Content matters enormously.

But I think structure matters at least as much, and we talk about it far less. By structure I mean: the shape of time. The rhythm of days. The pattern of tension and release, work and rest, anticipation and arrival. The way experiences are arranged relative to each other. The waiting between things.

Here's a simple example. Imagine two people who eat the exact same meal — same ingredients, same quality, same quantity. One of them eats it after not having eaten for sixteen hours. The other eats it two hours after a previous meal. The content is identical. The structure is completely different. And the experience of eating will be almost incomparably different, in ways that cannot be explained by the food itself.

Or imagine listening to a piece of music. The notes are the content. But music, more than almost any other art form, demonstrates that structure is not just the container for content — structure *is* content. The silence between notes is as much part of the music as the notes themselves. Rhythm is structure. Timing is structure. The way a phrase is delayed, withheld, finally resolved — that's not packaging. That's the music.

Waiting, in this framework, is structural. It's the silence between notes. And just as removing all the silences from a piece of music would not give you more music but would instead destroy the music entirely, a life without waiting — a life of pure continuous experience, stimulation, arrival — would not be a richer life. It would be a kind of impoverishment. The experiences would lose their edges. There would be no relief, no contrast, no anticipation. Everything would flatten into a continuous present tense, which is nobody's idea of paradise, despite how much we act as though it is.

---

I suspect this is part of what's happened to a lot of people in the era of smartphones, though I want to be careful not to turn this into a familiar techno-pessimism rant, because I don't entirely believe in that narrative. But I do think something true is contained in the observation that we have dramatically reduced the amount of waiting in daily life, and not just waiting for large things — waiting for letters, waiting for news, waiting to see a film you heard about until it came to a theater near you — but the micro-waiting. The thirty seconds at the bus stop where you used to simply stand and look at the street. The five minutes between tasks where you used to briefly exist without input. Those small intervals of structural nothing.

What has replaced them is not, I'd argue, more content. What has replaced them is the elimination of the gap, which is a structural change. And structural changes have structural consequences. If you remove all the silences from music, you don't get more music — you get noise. Not bad noise necessarily. Sometimes interesting noise. But it is no longer the same thing.

I'm not making a moral argument about phones. I use mine constantly and I don't feel particularly guilty about it. What I'm describing is more of a phenomenological observation: I think we may have altered, in a fairly significant way, our experience of time by eliminating the waiting, and we haven't fully reckoned with what we've lost by doing so. Not because waiting is inherently virtuous — it isn't, and plenty of waiting is just boring or painful or wasted — but because the structure of anticipation and arrival is genuinely constitutive of how meaning is created.

The feast requires the fast. Not always. Not as a rule. But as a condition of possibility. Appetite requires absence.

---

Here is something strange I believe: some of the richest moments in any life are not moments of experience but moments of pure anticipation. The night before the trip. The minutes before the concert starts, when the house lights are still up and the audience is still shuffling into seats. The morning of a day you've been looking forward to.

There's a particular sweetness to these moments that the experience itself often doesn't match. The trip will have frustrations; the concert will have a drunk person standing in front of you; the day will not go quite as you imagined. But the anticipation was perfect, because it was unconstrained. Anticipation is imagination operating within the warm glow of possibility. Everything is still open. The future is still a space rather than a sequence of actual events.

Children understand this instinctively. Children are often more excited about Christmas Eve than Christmas Day. They are masters of anticipation in a way that adults frequently lose, often because adults have accumulated enough experience to have lower confidence that things will be as good as they imagine. Disappointment, repeated often enough, clips the wings of anticipation. Adults become more conservative in their expectations, and as a consequence their waiting becomes more anxious and less pleasurable. This strikes me as a genuine loss — not inevitable, but very common.

I think the capacity to wait with pleasure rather than anxiety is a skill, and like most skills it can be practiced or neglected. It requires a kind of surrender to the present moment of not-yet-having that doesn't come naturally to many people. It requires trusting that the wanting itself has value, not just the getting. Which is easier to say than to feel, I know.

---

One of the things I find genuinely moving about old letters — the actual physical letters that people wrote to each other before any of this, before telegrams even, in the era when a letter might take weeks to arrive — is that you can sometimes feel, reading them, the structure of waiting that surrounded the writing. The writer knew they were putting words into a long silence. They knew that by the time this letter was read, circumstances would have changed in ways they couldn't predict. They were writing across an interval that was not empty but filled with invisible change.

There's a tenderness to that. There's a quality of address that feels different from any contemporary mode of communication. When you're writing an email, you half-expect a response within hours. When you're texting, within minutes. The interval is so small that the writing and the reading exist in something approaching the same moment, the same temporal context. But those letter writers were throwing words into the future, to a person they could imagine but not know in the moment of reading, in circumstances they could not see.

I think this gave some of those letters a particular depth. Not all of them — plenty of old letters are as trivial as any text message, and the idea that previous generations were deeper or more serious than us is mostly romanticized nonsense. But the form itself required a kind of long-term thinking. You couldn't dash off a quick follow-up if you forgot to say something. You had to hold things in mind. You had to be more complete. The waiting was not just between letters but within the letter itself, built into its construction.

---

I want to end with something smaller and less argued. More just noticed.

There's a specific moment in the late afternoon — and I realize this will resonate more for people who live in places with actual seasonal variation, but bear with me — somewhere around four or four-thirty on a winter day when the light has gone low and golden and then begins to fail. You're inside, and the inside is warm, and you can see through the window that the outside is cold and darkening. The day is done but the evening hasn't properly started. Dinner is not yet a thing. The night's particular quality hasn't arrived. You're in between.

This moment has a texture that I love in a way I can't fully explain. There's something completed about it — the day is effectively over, you've done or not done what you were going to do — and something unstarted. It's a threshold moment. A *madrugada* of the afternoon. You can sit in it, if you let yourself, without filling it. Just for a few minutes. Just with the low light and the warmth and the sense of being in a pause.

This is what I've been trying to circle around this whole time. Not waiting as endurance. Not waiting as the tedious space between real things. But waiting as a mode of presence. The pause that is not empty. The gap that holds something — not experience exactly, and not meaning in any articulable sense, but something that makes the experiences and meanings on either side of it more themselves.

The water trembling before it boils. The hour that belongs to no one. The night before the journey. The low gold light at four in the afternoon.

These are not nothing. These are something quite specific and quite precious. And I think we owe them, if not our full attention, at least a little more than we typically give.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
